channel.title: All Listings on SuperList.us
channel.description: Recent content in All Listings on SuperList.us

item.title: Pete & Jake's Hot Rod Parts
item.description: One of the most respected names in the hot rod world, operating out of Peculiar, Missouri since 1974. Born from a friendship forged while building two custom rides, Pete and Jake turned their passion into a parts and service empire. Car guys nationwide know this name. The Peculiar address just adds character.

item.title: Town of Dinosaur, Colorado
item.description: Originally called Artesia, this Colorado town renamed itself in 1966 to capitalize on its proximity to Dinosaur National Monument. They went all in — streets were renamed to things like Tyrannosaurus Trail, Brontosaurus Boulevard, and Antrodemus Alley. The commitment to the bit is honestly inspiring.

item.title: Accident, Maryland
item.description: Accident, Maryland, is a scenic small town in Garrett County. The name reportedly comes from a surveying mishap — two different parties accidentally claimed the same plot of land. The town leaned into it. It’s actually a beautiful mountain community, but good luck telling anyone where you live without them asking if you’re okay.

item.title: Hot Coffee, Mississippi
item.description: Hot Coffee, Mississippi, got its name from a traveler’s inn that served, you guessed it, really good hot coffee. A man named Davis brewed it with pure spring water and New Orleans beans, using molasses as a sweetener. People loved it so much they named the whole town after the drink. There is not currently a Starbucks.

item.title: Bat Cave, North Carolina
item.description: Bat Cave, North Carolina, is a real unincorporated community in Henderson County. Named after an actual bat cave in the nearby mountains. The mailing address is the best part — imagine putting “Bat Cave, NC” as your return address on every piece of mail you send. Bruce Wayne could never.

item.title: Truth or Consequences, New Mexico
item.description: In 1950, the host of the NBC game show “Truth or Consequences” promised to air an episode from the first town that renamed itself after the show. Hot Springs, New Mexico, took the deal. Over 70 years later, the name stuck and nobody changed it back. Locals call it “T or C” and the hot springs are still great.

item.title: Holiday World & Splashin' Safari
item.description: A full-sized theme park in Santa Claus, Indiana. Roller coasters, water slides, and free unlimited soft drinks — which is a genuinely aggressive perk. The park is themed around holidays: Christmas, Halloween, Thanksgiving, and the 4th of July. It’s been open since 1946, making it one of the oldest theme parks in the country.

item.title: Town of Chicken, Alaska
item.description: Chicken, Alaska is a surviving gold rush town with a summer population that occasionally cracks double digits. The story goes that residents wanted to name it “Ptarmigan” after the local bird, but nobody could agree on the spelling, so they just called it Chicken. There’s a gift shop, a café, and an amount of gold panning that is deeply optimistic.

item.title: Peculiar Games & Hobbies
item.description: Floor-to-ceiling shelves of board games, Warhammer 40K, Magic The Gathering, and more in Peculiar, Missouri. They have a game room where people drive from as far as Joplin for tournaments. The owners are famously friendly and will absolutely convince you to buy one more game than you planned on.

item.title: Peculiar Winery
item.description: A family-owned winery in Peculiar, Missouri, run by a guy who went from professional wrestling to television to wine. Handcrafted Missouri wines in a town whose official slogan is “Where the Odds Are With You.” The tasting room is open on weekends and the name has never once needed explaining.

item.title: Ding Dong, Texas
item.description: A small community in Bell County, Texas. Two men named Bell opened a store, hung a sign with two bells and the words “Ding Dong” on it, and that was that. The town has been Ding Dong ever since. There’s no doorbell joke that hasn’t already been made.

item.title: Carriage House Furnishings
item.description: Handcrafted hardwood furniture, rockers, hope chests, and rocking horses in a showroom that was once a real carriage restoration shop. Locally made in Lancaster County. The showroom still has historical items from the stagecoach era on display. Located in Intercourse, because of course it is.

item.title: Why, Arizona
item.description: Why, Arizona exists because the state requires town names to have at least three letters. The intersection of Routes 85 and 86 formed a “Y,” but a single letter wouldn’t do. So they named it “Why.” Population: about 116. The most frequently asked question when you tell someone where you live is, appropriately, “why?”

item.title: Mountain View Golf Club
item.description: A public golf course in Boring, Oregon with views of Mount Hood. Enough challenge for experienced players, approachable for newcomers. The Leaderboard Restaurant & Bar is on-site for post-round beverages. Telling people you golf in Boring is a conversation starter.

item.title: Keek's Treats & Café
item.description: A small café near the Boring Station Trailhead serving breakfast sandwiches, soups, cookies, brownies, and cinnamon rolls. Everything is made fresh and the portions are generous. The kind of place where the owner knows your name by your third visit.

item.title: Boring Bark & Garden Supply
item.description: Landscaping supply in Boring, Oregon. Bark, soil, gravel, and the origin story of Boring Bean Coffee, which literally started in their parking lot. Come for the mulch, stay for the vibes. Open seasonally.

item.title: Nothing, Arizona
item.description: Nothing, Arizona is a ghost town on US Route 93. At its peak, it had a gas station and a few residents. The welcome sign once read: “The staunch citizens of Nothing are full of Hope, Faith, and Believe in the Future of Nothing.” The gas station is closed. There is, quite literally, nothing here. And we listed it anyway.

item.title: Fox Meadows Creamery
item.description: Cow-to-cone ice cream made on-site with milk from their own small dairy farm. 24 flavors, a playground, a market with local foods, and an address that will make your GPS voice say something uncomfortable. Located in Intercourse, Pennsylvania, obviously.

item.title: Santa Claus Post Office
item.description: The post office in Santa Claus, Indiana, gets absolutely slammed every December with people mailing Christmas cards just so they get the “Santa Claus, IN” postmark. The town is also home to Holiday World theme park. Yes, the entire town’s personality is Christmas. Year-round.

item.title: Boring Brewing Company
item.description: Craft beer from Boring, Oregon. Every label writes itself. The town’s name is the best marketing department a brewery could ask for. Local, small-batch, and genuinely good — which is, frankly, a little on-the-nose for a town called Boring.

item.title: Sam & Ella's Chicken Palace
item.description: Multiple restaurants across the US have independently decided that naming a chicken restaurant after a food-borne illness is a power move. Say “Sam and Ella’s” out loud. Now say it faster. The food has good reviews, despite the name doing absolutely everything it can to undermine consumer confidence.

item.title: The Old Country Store
item.description: A landmark shop in Intercourse, PA, selling handmade Amish quilts, homemade jams, chow chow, Amish peanut butter mixed with marshmallow, and dog treats. One of those places where you walk in for one thing and leave with a bag full of stuff you didn’t know you needed.

item.title: Okay, OK Post Office
item.description: The post office in Okay, Oklahoma. It’s not great, it’s not terrible — it’s just Okay. The town was named after O.K. Trucks, a brand of oil tanker trucks manufactured there in 1919. Home to about 500 people. The zip code is 74446. The vibes are acceptable.

item.title: Liepold Farms
item.description: A family farm in Boring, Oregon, that’s been a fall tradition for generations. Pumpkin patches, corn mazes, hayrides, apple slingshots, and a children’s pavilion. Spring and summer bring berries. Everything about this place is wholesome and the address is hilarious.

item.title: Donkey Balls Original Factory & Store
item.description: A chocolate company on the Big Island of Hawaii that sells chocolate-covered macadamia nuts called, yes, Donkey Balls. The “Dirty Balls” variety is coated in cocoa powder. The branding is ridiculous on purpose and it has worked spectacularly. Free factory tours available.

item.title: Not So Boring Bar & Grill
item.description: A bar in Boring, Oregon, that decided to address the elephant in the room with its name. Live music, trivia nights, and a menu that proves small-town bars can do more than frozen mozzarella sticks. The name does the marketing for them.

item.title: W.L. Zimmerman & Sons Hardware
item.description: Serving the Intercourse community since 1909. Hardware, housewares, hunting and fishing licenses, propane, heating oil, UPS shipping, notarial services, and money orders. If you need it, Zimmerman’s probably has it. Your great-grandparents shopped here, and they turned out fine.

item.title: Kitchen Kettle Village
item.description: Over 40 local shops in one walkable village in the heart of Intercourse, Pennsylvania. Homemade jams, Amish quilts, handcrafted leather goods, and fresh shoofly pie. The name of the town gets all the attention, but the shopping keeps people coming back. Family-run since 1954.

item.title: North American Bigfoot Center
item.description: A museum in Boring, Oregon dedicated entirely to Bigfoot. Examine mysterious footprint casts, watch grainy footage of alleged sightings, and browse the merch shop. Whether you’re a true believer or a total skeptic, it’s one of the least boring things in Boring.

item.title: Boring Bean Coffee
item.description: A coffee roaster in Boring, Oregon, that is anything but boring. Started when they began roasting beans outside the door of their landscaping supply business, Boring Bark. Customers started showing up more for the coffee than the mulch. Fair trade, small batch, genuinely great coffee from a town that insists on underselling itself.
